name: Camazotz
description: Camazotz, meaning "death bat" or "bat of death" in the Kʼicheʼ Mayan language, is a fearsome bat god associated with night, death, and sacrifice. He is a prominent figure in Mayan mythology, particularly in the Popol Vuh, the sacred book of the Kʼicheʼ Maya. Camazotz is depicted as a monstrous creature with a bat's body and a human-like head, often carrying a sacrificial knife. His origins are believed to stem from actual giant vampire bats that once inhabited the Americas. He dwells in the underworld, Xibalba, and is considered a dangerous and malevolent entity. Camazotz's power lies in his ability to inflict harm and death, particularly through decapitation. He represents the primal fears associated with bats and the darkness. His role in the Popol Vuh involves testing the hero twins, Hunahpu and Xbalanque, during their descent into Xibalba. The twins were forced to spend the night in the House of Bats, where Camazotz decapitated Hunahpu. He is a potent symbol of the dangers lurking in the shadows and the inevitability of death. While generally viewed negatively, Camazotz also embodies transformation and the cycle of life and death, as bats themselves are creatures of the night that emerge at dusk and return to their dark lairs at dawn. He serves as a reminder of the constant presence of death within the world and the need to respect its power.
subtitle: Bat God of Death and Sacrifice

keyMyths.title: The House of Bats
keyMyths.description: Hunahpu and Xbalanque entered the House of Bats in Xibalba. Hunahpu was decapitated by Camazotz while waiting for dawn.

keyMyths.title: Hunahpu's Resurrection
keyMyths.description: Xbalanque later replaced Hunahpu's head with a squash. Eventually, his real head was restored, showing resilience against death.

keyMyths.title: Origin of Camazotz
keyMyths.description: Some believe Camazotz's mythology stems from giant vampire bats that once roamed the Americas. These real creatures fueled the fearsome image.

sources.author: Dennis Tedlock
